words.gloss: able to change to suit circumstances
able to adapt to many functions
determined not to change one's mind
having a range of different elements

items.explanation: Trap: 'compliant' sounds like a flexible, easygoing trait, tempting students to place it there, but it actually describes going along with rules/orders rather than adjusting oneself.

words.gloss: work together toward a shared goal
join with another to do something
pleasant and suited to working together
admit or give up after denying
suffer through something without giving up
defeat or gain control by force
make a great effort at something difficult
try very hard to achieve something
make an effort without guaranteeing success
commit to and begin a task
follow or work toward a goal
able to recover quickly and bounce back
reach a stable level after growth
lasting only a very short time
having no doubt something is true
too ready to believe without evidence

items.explanation: Trap: 'credulous' sounds positive like 'confident,' but it actually means gullible — believing too easily — the opposite of healthy certainty.

words.gloss: ready and happy to do something
having a strong desire to do something
hopeful and confident about the future

items.explanation: Trap: 'apathy' can look like a grim/negative-outlook word, but it belongs with unwillingness since it describes disengagement, not pessimism about the future.

power to give orders and enforce obedience

ruler with absolute, often cruel power
open to more than one interpretation
quality of being honest and straightforward
friendly, pleasant, easy to talk to
pleasant and suited to one's needs

items.explanation: Trap: 'cogent' sounds like a 'Skillful' word (clever speech), but it groups with honest/direct communication since it describes a convincing, clear argument, not cleverness itself.

polish until it shines; improve a reputation
shocking lack of shame in behavior

items.explanation: Trap: 'discreet' sounds shy/quiet like diffident/demure, which is correct here, but students often confuse it with the similar-sounding 'discrete' (meaning separate) — a classic SAT spelling trap.

items.explanation: Trap: 'abstain' looks like it means to hold back from danger, tempting a match with 'Impending Danger,' but it actually means choosing to refrain from an action, so it groups with the 'Shortened/Limited' cluster of restraint words.
